topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my screen, stark and unwelcome: '14 Willow Creek Lane - Now for Sale.' My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that tasted like dust. It was impossible. After all these years of moving, of building a completely different life, this address, this ghost of my youth, was suddenly up for bidding. I stared at the listing, my fingers trembling slightly over the screen. Every memory—the chipped paint on the porch railing, the way the afternoon sun slanted across the overgrown garden—surfaced with a stinging clarity. I felt a profound sense of inadequacy wash over me, a sudden, heavy weight settling in my chest. *What have I done?* I thought, the question echoing hollowly in the silence of my apartment. I quickly minimized the window, shoving the device into my pocket as if the very sight of it might contaminate me. I needed distance. I needed to pretend this intrusion wasn't a direct assault on the carefully constructed present.

[story 2]

I walked into the coffee shop, the noise of the espresso machine a frantic shield against the sudden, icy dread gripping my stomach. When I finally looked at my phone, the image of the old house stared back—a picture I hadn't seen in decades, now overlaid with a price tag and an 'Open House' date. My jaw tightened. I gripped my mug so hard the ceramic threatened to crack. It wasn't just nostalgia; it was a sudden, sharp awareness of the path I’d taken, the choices that had led me so far from that quiet, unremarkable place. A wave of shame washed over me, making my shoulders hunch inward. I mumbled an apology to the barista, a flimsy excuse for the tension radiating off me. I needed to erase the image, to scrub the unwelcome memory from my immediate perception, but the details—the exact shade of the faded blue shutters—were burned behind my eyelids.

[story 3]

The email landed with a dull thud in my inbox. 'For Sale: 22 Oak Street.' I didn't move for a full minute, just sitting there, the lukewarm tea in my hand suddenly feeling meaningless. My gaze drifted to the screen, and the familiar layout of the old living room—the worn rug, the window seat—seemed to mock my current, curated existence. A hot flush crept up my neck, spreading through my cheeks. It was as if the house was judging the life I'd built, finding it flimsy, temporary. I started pacing the small space, my steps quick and uneven, a restless energy fueled by a deep-seated feeling of falling short. *I should have stayed,* a voice whispered in the back of my mind, laced with a bitter resignation. I stopped abruptly, forcing a slow, deliberate breath, trying to smooth the agitated lines around my eyes, determined to appear composed, even as my insides felt tightly wound and brittle.
